region_of_origin: China (East Asia); widely cultivated across Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Hericium erinaceus raw mushroom in the clinical terminology; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw monkey head mushroom is approximately 80 g, equivalent to one medium-sized whole mushroom or a generous handful of trimmed pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Monkey Head Mushroom (Raw) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for raw monkey head mushroom (CR ID F14030004). Calcium reported as -1 (not available in source). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, sugar, cholesterol, and micronutrients beyond potassium and iron are not reported in the HPB entry; these are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Hericium erinaceus (FDC ID 169403) where available, and set to -1 where not available in either source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mushrooms — Analogous estimate based on low-GI mushroom category data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Hericium erinaceus (monkey head / lion's mane mushroom) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 28 is estimated from the closest analogous food category — edible mushrooms (oyster mushroom GI ~28, button mushroom GI ~10–15 range) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. A conservative mid-range value of 28 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 7.59 × 0.80) / 100 = 1.7 g. This is a low-GI, low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium and very high potassium (443 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (4.4 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Monkey head mushroom is an excellent DASH-diet food for hypertension management; its zero sodium and high potassium content (443 mg/100 g) support vasodilation and blood pressure reduction — avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~28), high dietary fibre (4.4 g/100 g), and beta-glucan content slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 28 and high dietary fibre including beta-glucans, monkey head mushroom supports glycaemic control and is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without added sugars or sweet sauces, consistent with ADA whole-food dietary principles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.26 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.4 g/100 g), and high potassium (443 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Monkey head mushroom's negligible fat, zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre, and potassium content make it cardioprotective; regular consumption as part of a plant-rich diet may support LDL reduction and arterial health per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan content modulating immune response and reducing inflammatory cytokines; high fibre supporting gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Monkey head mushroom's beta-glucans may modulate immune response and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines relevant to RA; prepare by steaming or stir-frying without deep-frying to preserve anti-inflammatory properties, and introduce gradually for individuals with mushroom sensitivities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Monkey head mushroom (Hericium erinaceus), also known as lion's mane mushroom, is a distinctive edible fungus characterised by its white, round, shaggy appearance with cascading spine-like projections resembling a lion's mane or a monkey's head. In its raw state it has a firm, spongy texture and a mild, slightly earthy flavour with subtle seafood-like undertones. It is widely used in Chinese and East Asian cuisine — both as a culinary ingredient and as a traditional medicinal food — and is increasingly popular globally for its purported neuroprotective and immune-modulating properties attributed to its beta-glucan and hericenone content. Nutritionally, raw monkey head mushroom is low in calories (43 kcal/100 g), low in fat (0.26 g/100 g), and provides moderate protein (2.5 g/100 g) with a notable dietary fibre content of 4.4 g/100 g. It is exceptionally rich in potassium (443 mg/100 g) and contains zero sodium, making it ideal for DASH-diet and hypertension management. Its low glycemic index (~28) and high beta-glucan fibre content support glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes. The mushroom is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly prepared by stir-frying, steaming, braising, or sautéing, and is used in soups, vegetarian dishes, and as a meat substitute due to its meaty texture when cooked.
common_user_queries: Is monkey head mushroom good for diabetes?
What is lion's mane mushroom nutrition?
Is monkey head mushroom suitable for hypertension?
What is 猴头菇 in English?
How to cook monkey head mushroom?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F14030004) with confidence 0.95. Fatty acid sub-fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Hericium erinaceus, FDC 169403). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category data for edible mushrooms, clearly documented. Ontology anchors are defensible and not invented.
notes: calcium_mg is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source (F14030004) does not report a calcium value for this food item; no reliable calcium figure for raw Hericium erinaceus was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 169403 either — the USDA entry reports calcium as 0 mg which may reflect below-detection-threshold rather than true zero, so -1 is retained to avoid misrepresentation. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source; USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 88.3 g/100 g for Hericium erinaceus but this value is not confirmed in the HPB record and is therefore not carried over to avoid source mixing without explicit reconciliation. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food item; detailed micronutrient data for Hericium erinaceus is limited in both HPB and USDA SR Legacy databases as of 2026. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on edible mushroom category data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Hericium erinaceus exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium is reported as 0.0 mg in the HPB source — confirmed zero per approved source. Trans fat reported as 0.0 — naturally zero for a raw fungal food with negligible fat content. Vitamin B12 reported as 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based fungal food with no animal-derived components.
